message.content:
Below is a launch-audit checklist for **network / delivery** performance, using Lighthouse’s official audit names/IDs plus the relevant web standards for HTTP behavior. I’ve grouped each item by what you should verify and what standard or guidance it maps to.

| Check | Lighthouse audit ID | What to verify | Standards / official guidance |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Text compression** | `uses-text-compression` | HTML, CSS, JS, SVG, and other text assets are served with **gzip** or **brotli** content-encoding when appropriate. | HTTP content-coding is defined by HTTP semantics, which recognizes `Content-Encoding` and registered codings such as `gzip` and `br`.[RFC 9110](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9110) |
| **Efficient cache lifetime / HTTP caching** | `uses-long-cache-ttl` | Static resources have a **long-lived cache policy** so repeat visits avoid re-downloading unchanged assets. | `Cache-Control` is the core HTTP cache directive set; origin servers should use explicit caching directives and validators where appropriate.[RFC 9111](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9111) |
| **HTTP/2** | `uses-http2` | The origin serves page resources over **HTTP/2** rather than HTTP/1.1 where possible. | HTTP/2 is standardized in RFC 9113.[RFC 9113](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9113) |
| **HTTP/3** | *No single Lighthouse performance audit ID in the classic report* | Prefer **HTTP/3** where your CDN/origin and clients support it, especially for connection setup and multiplexing improvements. | HTTP/3 is standardized in RFC 9114.[RFC 9114](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9114) |
| **Preconnect** | `uses-rel-preconnect` | Use `<link rel="preconnect">` only for truly critical third-party origins to warm up DNS, TCP, and TLS early. | `preconnect` is defined by the HTML spec as a link type / resource hint.[HTML Living Standard](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/) |
| **Preload** | `uses-rel-preload` | Use `<link rel="preload">` for high-priority resources that are required for initial render and would otherwise be discovered too late. | `preload` is defined by the HTML spec as a resource hint.[HTML Living Standard](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/) |
| **DNS prefetch** | *No dedicated classic Lighthouse performance audit ID* | Use `<link rel="dns-prefetch">` for low-cost speculative DNS resolution when you cannot justify a full preconnect. | `dns-prefetch` is supported as a resource hint in the HTML spec and browser documentation.[HTML Living Standard](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/) |
| **Prefetch** | *No dedicated classic Lighthouse performance audit ID* | Use `<link rel="prefetch">` only for likely-next navigations or future subresources, not for current critical path assets. | `prefetch` is a standard resource hint in HTML.[HTML Living Standard](https://html.spec.whatwg.org/) |
| **CDN usage** | *No dedicated classic Lighthouse performance audit ID* | Serve static and geographically distributed assets from a **CDN** when it reduces latency and improves cache locality. | web.dev recommends evaluating CDN usage during performance audits and testing caching / TTFB characteristics.[Audit performance on web.dev](https://web.dev/articles/performance-audit) |
| **Server response time / TTFB** | `server-response-time` | Reduce **Time to First Byte** by improving backend latency, edge caching, request processing, and infrastructure efficiency. | web.dev identifies server response time / TTFB as a key audit area for performance work.[Audit performance on web.dev](https://web.dev/articles/performance-audit) |
| **Redirects** | `redirects` | Minimize redirect chains, especially on the initial navigation, because each redirect adds extra round trips and delays the start of render. | web.dev includes redirects in Lighthouse performance checks for launch audits.[Audit performance on web.dev](https://web.dev/articles/performance-audit) |
| **Keep-alive** | *No dedicated classic Lighthouse performance audit ID* | Ensure HTTP connections are reused with persistent connections so subsequent requests do not pay repeated handshake cost. | HTTP/1.1 persistent connections are standardized in RFC 9112, and HTTP/2/3 reuse a single connection for multiplexed streams.[RFC 9112](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9112)[RFC 9113](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9113)[RFC 9114](https://www.rfc-editor.org/rfc/rfc9114) |

A few important clarifications:

- **Lighthouse audit IDs** are stable for the classic audit report, but some network/delivery best practices are **not surfaced as a dedicated audit** and instead appear as broader diagnostics, opportunities, or are better covered by manual review.[Chrome Lighthouse overview](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/overview)
- **HTTP/3** and **keep-alive** are important launch checks, but they are typically **standards/architecture checks**, not a one-to-one classic Lighthouse audit.
- For **resource hints**, Lighthouse’s classic performance audits explicitly cover **preconnect** and **preload**, while **dns-prefetch** and **prefetch** are generally manual or framework-level checks rather than dedicated classic performance audit IDs.[Chrome Lighthouse overview](https://developer.chrome.com/docs/lighthouse/overview)

If you want, I can turn this into a **launch audit template** with columns for **pass/fail criteria**, **how to test**, and **recommended thresholds** for each item.
